coredata.dc:title: Remarkable fluorescence enhancement of acridine orange and rhodamine B through immobilization on zirconia nanotube array film and its application on nitrite sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Dong
Wang, Xixin
Liu, Dongxin
Zhao, Jianling
coredata.dc:description: Zirconia nanotube array films (ZNAF) prepared by anodic oxidation method were used as immobilization materials for acridine orange (AO), rhodamine B (RB) and AO-RB systems. A comparative study on their fluorescence emission intensity, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and fluorescence detection of nitrite in aqueous solutions and on immobilization films with ZNAF as carriers was carried out. Results demonstrate that the solution pH values and immobilization on ZNAF have a great influence on the performance of these fluorescent molecules. Compared with aqueous solutions, the fluorescence emission intensity of AO and RB is considerably increased by immobilization, which is 8.0 and 4.2 times higher than the original, respectively. The energy transfer efficiency (E) of the AO-RB system increases from 40.9 % to 84.8 % by loading it on ZNAF. Moreover, after immobilization onto ZNAF, the fluorescence detection performance of nitrite is also significantly improved. The limit of detection decreases from 0.95 ng/mL to 0.22 ng/mL and the sensitivity increases from 939.18 to 15,031.68 mL/µg through loading AO onto ZNAF.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZrO2 nanotube arrays
Acridine orange
Rhodamine B
Nitrite
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of zirconia nanotube array films
Preparation of fluorescent molecule aqueous solutions
Fluorescence performance of acridine orange before and after immobilization
Fluorescence performance of rhodamine B before and after immobilization
FRET performance of AO-RB system before and after immobilization
Fluorescence detection performance of nitrite before and after immobilization
The regeneration and reusability of the carrier

(a) Excitation spectra, (b, c) emission spectra at different pH values and (d) emission intensity vs. pH curves of AO-aq and AO/ZNAF.

head.ce:title: Remarkable fluorescence enhancement of acridine orange and rhodamine B through immobilization on zirconia nanotube array film and its application on nitrite sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zirconia nanotube array films (ZNAF) prepared by anodic oxidation method were used as immobilization materials for acridine orange (AO), rhodamine B (RB) and AO-RB systems. A comparative study on their fluorescence emission intensity, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and fluorescence detection of nitrite in aqueous solutions and on immobilization films with ZNAF as carriers was carried out. Results demonstrate that the solution pH values and immobilization on ZNAF have a great influence on the performance of these fluorescent molecules. Compared with aqueous solutions, the fluorescence emission intensity of AO and RB is considerably increased by immobilization, which is 8.0 and 4.2 times higher than the original, respectively. The energy transfer efficiency (E) of the AO-RB system increases from 40.9 % to 84.8 % by loading it on ZNAF. Moreover, after immobilization onto ZNAF, the fluorescence detection performance of nitrite is also significantly improved. The limit of detection decreases from 0.95 ng/mL to 0.22 ng/mL and the sensitivity increases from 939.18 to 15,031.68 mL/µg through loading AO onto ZNAF.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic fluorescent molecules are characterized by high selectivity, sensitivity and fluorescence quantum yield and have essential applications in various fields, such as disease diagnosis [1–3], photocatalysis [4–6], ion detection [7–9] and photoelectric sensing [10–12].

The common application form of organic fluorescent molecules is their solutions. The solvation of fluorescent molecules in various solvents is different [13], and the pH values of the solution also change the ionization structure of fluorescent molecules [14,15]. Therefore, these factors will cause fluorescent molecular solutions to exhibit different fluorescence properties. For example, the aggregation degree and fluorescence properties of acridine orange (AO) are influenced by the concentration of potassium halide or sodium halide in aqueous solution [16]. The colors of the coumarin-fluorescein system change with different pH values [17].

Studies have shown that dispersing organic fluorescent molecules on carrier materials (immobilization) can eliminate the influence of solvation and thus improve the fluorescence performance of fluorescent molecules [18,19]. The film carriers offer several advantages over the powder carriers, including a stable structure, easy device, convenient use and recycling [20]. Furthermore, the type and morphology of the carrier have a significant effect on the performance of the fluorescent molecular immobilization materials. For example, RB/NP and RB/NW composite films were prepared by immobilizing rhodamine B (RB) onto alumina nanopore array (NP) and alumina nanowire (NW) films, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of RB/NW is significantly higher than that of RB/NP, and RB/NW composite film exhibits an emission peak at 571 nm, with a peak width ranging from 525 to 650 nm [21]. On the other hand, the RB/ZnO nanowire composite film shows an emission peak at 495 nm, with a peak width spanning from 300 to 700 nm [22]. The RB/poly(methyl methacrylate) composite film shows the highest absorbance at 480–600 nm [23], while the cellulose-based microgel fluorescent film prepared with RB and cellulose glucose exhibits excellent light absorbing properties in the ultraviolet range (200–335 nm) [24]. Transparent glass [25] and fluorine-doped tin oxide glass [26] can also be used as carriers to prepare RB composite fluorescent films, whose transparency in the visible region is 24 % and 82 %, respectively.

AO and RB are widely used in many fields due to their mature preparation technology, low price and stable performance [27–29]. For instance, AO or RB is used as fluorescence probes for nitrite detection [30,31]. Ratiometric fluorescent probes prepared from RB and boron dipyrromethene are applicable for detecting Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions [32]. AO can be utilized not only for treating cancer and skin diseases [33] but also for the catalytic thiolation of coumarin rings in sunlight [34]. Additionally, AO and its derivatives can be used as ultraviolet absorbers [35] and fluorescent stains [36]. The emission spectrum of AO significantly overlaps with the excitation spectrum of RB, thereby facilitating the formation of a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system between AO and RB [37].

Zirconia is a vital carrier material with abundant resources, low cost, high refractive index and stable photochemical properties [38]. ZrO2 nanotube array films (ZNAF), prepared by anodic oxidation method, with highly ordered structures, large specific surface areas and more abundant surface defect states [39,40], can provide more active sites and exhibit excellent performance in many aspects, such as oral medicine [41,42], photocatalytic [43], hydrophobic coating [44] and metal detection [45]. However, there is still a lack of research on the performance enhancement of ZNAF for AO and AO-RB systems.

Water is the most widely used solvent because of its cost-effectiveness and eco-friendliness. In this paper, the AO and RB were immobilized on ZNAF to prepare composite fluorescent films. Fluorescence and FRET performances of the fluorescent films and their corresponding aqueous solutions were comparatively studied. Furthermore, the effect of solution pH values on the properties of the relevant samples was investigated. The study demonstrates that ZNAF can effectively improve and enhance the fluorescence emission intensity of AO and RB, as well as the performance of fluorescence detection of nitrite. Meanwhile, this work can also expand the potential applications of ZNAF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of zirconia nanotube array films
ce:section.ce:para: Zirconium sheets sized in 15 mm × 30 mm × 0.3 mm were placed in a mixture of deionized water and ethanol for ultrasonic cleaning, then rinsed with deionized water and dried. Next, 0.2 g ammonium fluoride and 2.1 g ammonium sulfate were added to the mixed solution of 16 g water and 64 g glycerol to obtain the electrolyte solution. With Zr sheet as anode and Pt sheet as cathode, the anodic oxidation reaction was performed for 60 min at 30 °C and 50 V under stirring. After anodic oxidation, the samples were washed and calcined at 400 °C for 2 h to obtain ZNAF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent molecule aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Britton-Robinson buffer solutions of pH = 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11, 0.05 M sulfuric acid solution (pH = 1) and 0.1 M sodium hydroxide solution (pH = 13) were prepared with corresponding reagents and deionized water. Acridine orange aqueous solution (AO-aq) and rhodamine B aqueous solution (RB-aq) at a concentration of 5 mg/L were prepared with the above aqueous solutions of different pH values.
Aqueous solutions in different pH values containing both AO and RB (AO-RB-aq) were prepared and the concentration of both AO and RB was 5 mg/L.

ZNAF samples were placed horizontally in weighing bottles, and 100 µL of the fluorescent molecular aqueous solution was evenly added to the samples. The weighing bottles were left in a closed dark place for 30 min, and then the samples were dried at 100 °C for 30 min to obtain composite fluorescent films of fluorescent molecules and ZNAF. The composite fluorescent films prepared with AO-aq, RB-aq and AO-RB-aq were labeled as AO/ZNAF, RB/ZNAF and AO-RB/ZNAF, respectively.
The schematic diagram of the preparation process for relevant samples is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The morphology, crystal structure and chemical composition of ZNAF carrier were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 450 FEG, FEI), X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Discover, Bruker) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The functional groups of the relevant samples were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, VECTOR22, Bruker). The fluorescence performance of the samples was tested under the same measurement conditions with a slit width of 2.5 nm and PMT voltage of 700 V at room temperature using the fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi). The fluorescence intensity of each sample was measured at three different locations and determined by averaging the test results. The fluorescence spectra of aqueous samples were measured using a quartz cuvette with a 2 mm optical path.

Fig. 2 depicts the surface and cross-sectional morphologies of ZNAF. The nanotubes are uniformly distributed and the nanotube mouths adhere together at the surface (Fig. 2(a)). The tube walls are rough, and the nanotubes are highly ordered and closely arranged, with a diameter of about 118 nm (Fig. 2(b)).

Fig. 3 (a) gives the XRD pattern of ZNAF. It can be seen from Fig. 3(a) that the crystalline phase of ZNAF consists of t-ZrO2 (PDF card No. 50-1089) and m-ZrO2 (PDF card No. 37-1484), with t-ZrO2 being the predominant phase for approximately 66.1 % of the total content. In addition, weaker Zr (PDF card No. 5-665) substrate diffraction peaks can also be observed in the pattern. The XPS spectra of ZNAF are shown in Fig. 3(b–d). As seen from Fig. 3(b), ZNAF is mainly composed of Zr and O. The peaks of Zr 3d located at 182.5 and 184.9 eV correspond to the binding energies of Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2 of Zr4+, indicating that Zr element exists in the form of +4 valence (Fig. 3(c)) [46]. The binding energy of O 1s is 530.4 eV, which is consistent with the peak position of the oxygen element in the metal oxide (Fig. 3(d)). XPS results demonstrate that ZNAF consists of ZrO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence performance of acridine orange before and after immobilization
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation spectra of AO-aq and AO/ZNAF at pH = 7 are shown in Fig. 4 (a). As seen from Fig. 4(a), excitation spectra of AO-aq and AO/ZNAF exhibit similar shapes, with the most prominent excitation peak at 490 nm. Notably, the excitation intensity of AO/ZNAF is significantly higher than that of AO-aq. The emission spectra of AO-aq and AO/ZNAF at different pH values are shown in Fig. 4(b, c). Their emission intensity vs. pH curves (shown in Fig. 4(d)) can be obtained from Fig. 4(b, c). It can be seen from Fig. 4(d) that the emission intensity of AO-aq and AO/ZNAF increases first and then decreases with increasing pH values. AO-aq exhibits the highest fluorescence intensity at pH = 3, whereas AO/ZNAF shows the highest fluorescence intensity at pH = 5. According to the maximum fluorescence intensity value of AO/ZNAF and AO-aq, the emission intensity of AO/ZNAF is approximately 8.0 times higher than that of AO-aq.

The results in Fig. 4 demonstrate that immobilization of AO with ZNAF significantly enhances the fluorescence property of AO. Moreover, the pH values of the solution greatly influence the fluorescence intensity of samples.

The molecular structures of AO at different pH values are shown in Fig. 5 . AO contains one pyridine N and two dimethylamine groups. In weak acidic aqueous solution, due to the protonation salt formation of pyridine N and some dimethylamine groups, AO can be better dissolved and dispersed in aqueous solution and exhibit stronger fluorescence emission. As the pH value decreases and the acidity of the solution increases, the protonation degree of the dimethylamine group is amplified, which leads to a decrease in the electron cloud density and a weakening of the fluorescence emission intensity of the AO molecules. In addition, excessive acidity also protonates the unsaturated carbon-nitrogen double bond of AO, inducing the central carbon atom to transform from sp2 to sp3 hybridization and form positive carbon ions. As a result, the large conjugate structure of the AO molecule is disrupted, which leads to a significant decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity. When the solution is alkaline, the protonation of AO decreases with the increase of pH values. Although the electron cloud density of molecules can rise, the solubility dispersion of AO becomes poor and the AO molecules tend to agglomerate with each other, causing a decrease in fluorescence emission intensity. When the solution is excessively alkaline, the agglomerated AO micelles can augment and precipitate microscopically [47], which reduces the fluorescence emission intensity significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence performance of rhodamine B before and after immobilization
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (a) gives the excitation spectra of RB-aq and RB/ZNAF at pH = 7. The strongest excitation peak of RB-aq occurs at 554 nm, whereas that of RB/ZNAF is observed at 552 nm and exhibits a slight blue shift. The excitation spectra of RB-aq and RB/ZNAF are similar in shape, with RB/ZNAF having significantly higher excitation intensity than RB-aq. The emission spectra of RB-aq and RB/ZNAF at different pH values are shown in Fig. 6(b, c), and their emission intensity vs. pH curves (shown in Fig. 6(d)) can be obtained from Fig. 6(b, c). From Fig. 6(d), it can be seen that the emission intensity vs. pH curves for RB-aq and RB/ZNAF exhibit a similar variation trend, with their maximum fluorescence emission intensity occurring at pH = 7. The emission intensity of RB/ZNAF is approximately 4.2 times higher than that of RB-aq.
The results in Fig. 6 illustrate that ZNAF also has a significant improvement in the fluorescence performance of RB, but the increase extent is lower than AO.

As seen from Fig. 6, the fluorescence intensity of RB-aq and RB/ZNAF vary with pH values. Fig. 7 shows molecular structures of RB at different pH values. Known from Fig. 7, RB contains one carboxylate and two diethylamine groups in the neutral aqueous solution. Since the diethylamine group exists as ammonium salt, RB can be better dissolved and dispersed in the neutral solution, thus displaying powerful fluorescence emission performance. As the pH value of the solution decreases and the acidity increases, the ionization of carboxyl groups is inhibited, leading to the decreased electron cloud density and the weakened fluorescence emission intensity of RB. When the solution is basic, the protonation of diethylamine groups is diminished. Although the electron cloud density of RB molecules increases, the solubility dispersion of RB becomes poor and the RB molecules tend to agglomerate with each other, which reduces the fluorescence emission intensity of RB accordingly [48].

The infrared spectra of AO/ZNAF and RB/ZNAF were measured to investigate the reason for the substantial increase in their fluorescence intensity, and the corresponding results are depicted in Fig. 8 . Compared with the individual IR spectrum of AO and ZNAF, the absorption peaks of ZNAF at 1620 and 1340 cm−1 as well as the vibration peaks of AO within the range of 400–1000 cm−1 disappear in the IR spectrum of AO/ZNAF (Fig. 8(a)). Similarly, the vibration peaks of RB at 400–1000 cm−1 also almost disappear in RB/ZNAF (Fig. 8(b)). Moreover, the vibration peak of C=O in RB is located at 1695 cm−1, while it blue-shifts to 1757 cm−1 in RB/ZNAF [49]. Compared with RB, the relative intensity of the characteristic vibration peak in RB/ZNAF changes significantly. The results in Fig. 8 demonstrate that the dispersion states of AO and RB in AO/ZNAF and RB/ZNAF exhibit significant differences from those in AO and RB. In AO and RB, fluorescent molecules themselves exhibit severe agglomeration. After immobilization onto ZNAF, the agglomeration states of AO and RB in AO/ZNAF and RB/ZNAF are significantly improved.

As mentioned above, ZNAF has a large specific surface area and its surface contains many polar sites such as hydroxyl groups and defects. AO and RB contain amino and carboxyl groups that strongly interact with the ZNAF surface. This interaction effectively inhibits the agglomeration of AO and RB, thus promoting high dispersion and fixation of AO and RB on the ZNAF surface. Meanwhile, immobilization onto ZNAF can eliminate the effect of solvation of water molecules. The above two factors contribute to the substantial enhancement of the fluorescence emission intensity of AO/ZNAF and RB/ZNAF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET performance of AO-RB system before and after immobilization
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Fig. 4(a), the strongest excitation peaks of AO-aq and AO/ZNAF are located at 490 nm. Therefore, the emission spectra of AO-RB-aq and AO-RB/ZNAF were examined under excitation light at 490 nm, and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 9 (a, b). Known from Fig. 9 (a, b), the emission spectra of the AO-RB system are superimposed by the emission spectra of AO and RB, and their relative intensities vary with pH values. The actual emission intensities I AO(AO-RB) and I RB(AO-RB) of AO and RB can be obtained by fitting mathematically the emission spectra of AO-RB-aq and AO-RB/ZNAF. I AO(AO-RB) vs. pH curves and I RB(AO-RB) vs. pH curves of AO-RB-aq and AO-RB/ZNAF can be obtained from Fig. 9(a, b), and results are shown in Fig. 9 (c, d). As shown in Fig. 9(c, d), I AO(AO-RB) vs. pH curves of AO-RB-aq and AO-RB/ZNAF exhibit similar trends, with their maximum values observed at pH = 3 and pH = 5, respectively. In addition, I RB(AO-RB) values of AO-RB-aq and AO-RB/ZNAF reach the maximum at pH = 7 and pH = 5, respectively.

Fig. 10 (a) shows the emission spectra of RB/ZNAF at 490 nm excitation wavelength. I RB vs. pH curves of RB/ZNAF (shown in Fig. 10(b)) can be obtained from Fig. 10(a). Similarly, as shown in Fig. 10(b), I RB vs. pH curves of RB-aq at 490 nm excitation wavelength are also obtained by the same test method.

The energy transfer efficiency E vs. pH curves of AO-RB-aq and AO-RB/ZNAF (E = 1–I AO(AO-RB)/I AO, Fig. 10(c)) were calculated from Figs. 4(d) and 9(c). The enhancement ratio vs. pH curves for each sample (enhancement ratio = I RB(AO-RB)/I RB, Fig. 10(d)) were calculated from Figs. 9(d) and 10(b). As shown in Fig. 10(c), the E values of AO-RB/ZNAF are significantly higher than that of AO-RB-aq, and their E vs. pH curves display similar trends in a pH range from 1 to 9. When the pH value increases from 11 to 13, E values of AO-RB-aq decrease, while E values of AO-RB/ZNAF increase significantly with a maximum E value of 84.8 % (pH = 13). As depicted in Fig. 10(d), the enhancement ratio of AO-RB/ZNAF is notably higher than that of AO-RB-aq and the maximum enhancement ratio of AO-RB/ZNAF is 5.3 at pH = 5.

Results in Fig. 10(c, d) demonstrate that the FRET performance of the AO-RB system can be significantly improved by loading AO-RB onto ZNAF. The curves in Fig. 10(c, d) are not fully correlated, mainly caused by the different utilization rates of the acceptor RB to the transferred energy of the donor AO under the different dispersion states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection performance of nitrite before and after immobilization
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite as a preservative is widespread in food and water, which has the potential to induce irreversible transformation of hemoglobin and possesses carcinogenic properties [50]. Prevention of the potential harm of nitrite (NO2 –) depends on its accurate detection [51]. There are various methods to detect NO2 – ions, among which fluorescence detection methods are characterized by simplicity, selectivity and high sensitivity.

It has been demonstrated in the literature that under medium strong acid conditions, NO2 – ions can catalyze the oxidation reaction between potassium bromate and fluorescent probe, leading to fluorescence quenching [31,52,53]. Based on this reaction, fluorescence detection of NO2 – ions can be achieved. Through the optimization of relevant process parameters, the experimental procedures for fluorescence detection of NO2 – ions with AO are as follows:

The tube was filled with 0.5 mL of phosphoric acid aqueous solution (1.0 M), 0.5 mL of potassium bromate aqueous solution (2 mM), 0.5 mL of AO aqueous solution (10 mg/L) and 0.5 mL of NO2 – ions aqueous solution. After thorough shaking, the tube was placed at room temperature in a closed dark environment for 15 min. Subsequently, 0.5 mL of NaOH aqueous solution (1.1 M) was added and shaken to obtain the test solution (labeled as AO-w). According to the method in Section 2.3, AO-w and ZNAF were used to prepare test samples (labeled as AO@ZNAF).

As depicted in Figs. 4 and 6, the fluorescence intensity of AO and RB is relatively strong at pH values ranging from 5 to 7. To mitigate the negative effect of pH value and improve the detection performance, NaOH solution was added to the test solution after the reaction. There are two purposes for this step. Firstly, the oxidation reaction can be terminated. Secondly, the pH value of the test solution can be adjusted to be close to neutral (approximately 5.9).

The emission spectra of AO-w and AO@ZNAF were tested at different NO2 – ions concentrations (
C
NO
2 −
), and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 11 (a, c). Let I 0 represent the fluorescence emission intensity of the blank sample (
C
NO
2 −
= 0), I denote the fluorescence emission intensity of the test sample, and ΔI indicates the difference in fluorescence intensity between the two samples (ΔI = I 0−I). The fitting lines of ΔI vs.
C
NO
2 −
for AO-w and AO@ZNAF (Fig. 11(b, d)) can be obtained from Fig. 11(a, c). As can be seen from Fig. 11(b, d), the ΔI and
C
NO
2 −
of AO-w and AO@ZNAF exhibit excellent linearity within the concentration range of 0.001 to 0.1 µg/mL, and the detailed performance parameters are listed in Table 1 .

The detection experiment was conducted with RB instead of AO in a similar way as above. The detection solution was designated as RB-w, while the detection sample with ZNAF as carriers was labeled as RB@ZNAF. The emission spectra and ΔI vs.
C
NO
2 −
fitting lines of RB-w and RB@ZNAF are given in Fig. 12 , and the specific performance parameters for detection are presented in Table 1.

The AO aqueous solution was replaced with an aqueous solution containing 10 mg/L AO and 10 mg/L RB, and the detection experiment was performed following the same procedure as described above. The detection solution was designated as AO-RB-w, while the corresponding detection sample with ZNAF as carriers was labeled as AO-RB@ZNAF. Fig. 13 (a) shows the emission spectra of AO-RB-w at different
C
NO
2 −
. Known from Fig. 13(a), the emission intensities of AO and RB (I AO and I RB) exhibit a decrease with increasing
C
NO
2 −
. Due to the minimal range of variation in I AO/I RB values with
C
NO
2 −
, it is difficult to detect NO2 – ions based on the relation between I AO/I RB and
C
NO
2 −
. The ΔI AO values are significantly higher than ΔI RB, showing better detection performance, and the ΔI AO vs.
C
NO
2 −
fitting line of AO-RB-w is shown in Fig. 13(b). The emission spectra of AO-RB@ZNAF (Fig. 13(c)) are similar to those of AO-RB-w (Fig. 13(a)). However, the values of ΔI RB are much greater than ΔI AO, indicating the possible superior detection performance according to the change of ΔI RB. The fitting line of ΔI RB vs.
C
NO
2 −
for AO-RB@ZNAF is given in Fig. 13(d), and the detection performance parameters obtained from Fig. 13 are presented in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The regeneration and reusability of the carrier
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the regeneration and reusability performance of the carriers, the AO@ZNAF composite film with the best detection performance was selected for the ZNAF regeneration experiment under the condition of 0.06 µg/mL nitrite concentrations. The regeneration steps are as follows: The AO@ZNAF sample was firstly immersed in 15 mL acetic acid solution (1.2 %) for 30 min, and then the acetic acid solution was renewed and the AO@ZNAF was immersed for another 30 min. Subsequently, the sample was immersed in 15 mL of deionized water for 30 min, followed by renewing the deionized water and immersing the sample for another 30 min. Finally, the sample was dried at 130 °C to obtain the regenerated ZNAF carrier.

The composite films were prepared by using the regenerated ZNAF carrier under the same conditions, and the properties of the composite films were compared when the regeneration times of the ZNAF carriers were different. The experimental results of carrier regeneration and reuse of AO@ZNAF are shown in Fig. 14 . It can be seen that there is no fluorescence emission in the regenerated ZNAF. However, the AO@ZNAF prepared using the regenerated ZNAF exhibits strong fluorescence emission with an emission intensity comparable to that of the initial sample, and the detection performance does not change significantly. After regenerating the ZNAF carriers five times, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the emission intensities for AO@ZNAF is 3.1 %. The results in Fig. 14 demonstrate that ZNAF carriers can be regenerated and reused.

The difference in solubility and ionization structure of AO and RB in aqueous solutions at different pH values changes their fluorescence intensity. AO exhibits the highest fluorescence intensity in an aqueous solution at pH = 3, whereas RB shows the highest fluorescence intensity in an aqueous solution at pH = 7. Immobilizing AO and RB onto ZNAF can eliminate the effect of solvation and significantly improve their dispersion state, which results in an increase in fluorescence emission intensity of 4.2–8.0 times higher than the original. In addition, the energy transfer efficiency of the AO-RB system is increased by over a factor of 1, and the enhancement ratio is about 4.6 times that of the corresponding aqueous solution.

The fluorescence detection method for nitrite has been optimized and the detection performance of AO as a fluorescent probe shows significant superiority over RB and the AO-RB system. Moreover, the detection performance is substantially improved by loading the test solution onto ZNAF. AO@ZNAF exhibits the best performance for the detection of NO2 – ions, with a minimum detection limit of 0.22 ng/mL and a linear range of 0.001–0.1 µg/mL. The carrier ZNAF can be regenerated and reused. This study can provide an essential reference for improving and enhancing the application performance of organic fluorescent molecules.

Dong Liu: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Xixin Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Dongxin Liu: Methodology, Validation. Jianling Zhao: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51972095).
